title: 2025/12/01 - SSC (Part 3)
displayTitle: 2025-12-01 - Social Services and Community Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of Integrity Sport Commission [Part 3]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of Integrity Sport Commission
description: Monday 1 December 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee 2024/25 Annual review of Integrity Sport Commission Hearing of evidence: Integrity Sport Commission Don Mackinnon, Chair Rebecca Rolls, Chief Executive Kim Rex, Manager System Performance, Ministry for Culture and Heritage 2024/25 Annual review of Sport New Zealand Hearing of evidence: Sport and Recreation New Zealand Raelene Castle, Chief Executive, Sport NZ Group Duane Kale, Acting Chair, Sport NZ and High-Performance Sport NZ Boards Julie Morrison, Group Manager - Strategy, Policy and Investment
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of Integrity Sport Commission. Integrity Sport Commission. Don Mackinnon, Chair

chunks.text: Well, good afternoon. Welcome to the Social Services Community Select Committee. Uh, thank you for being here this afternoon. So, uh, what I might do is, uh, ask you to introduce yourself and then, um, make some opening comments, wish, and then we'll move to questions from members. Great. Thank you. Um, good afternoon everyone. Uh, I'm John McKinnon and, uh, chair of the Sport Integrity Commission. Uh, Koto. I'm Rebecca, roll Chief Executive of the Sport Integrity Commission.

Okay. Um, thank you Mr. Chair. Koto. Uh, good afternoon. Members of the committee, um, uh, sport Integrity Commission, t Kahu Rani, uh, was established on the 1st of July, 2024. So we are reviewing today our very first year of, uh, operation.

To give you a very brief history, the commission was established as I'm sure some of you're aware, on, on the, on the back of numerous reviews into primarily into sport. Um, and, uh, those reviews had dealt with some very serious wrongdoing. Uh, and one of the con constant themes emerging from those reviews was the need for, uh, transparent, independent body free of sport New Zealand, with the resources to help sport and recreation, particularly when dealing with difficult trauma related, uh, issues.

So that was our mandate. Um, the integrity, sport and recreation, uh, sorry, the Integrity Sport, uh, commission Act was passed, uh, last, uh, in in 2024. Our mandate is set by that legislation. We deal with anti-doping, we deal with competition manipulation. We deal with participant protection, which is very broadly defined and includes bullying, harassment, discrimination, uh, sexual misconduct.

We deal, deal with CHI children safeguarding, and we deal with corruption and, uh, across the sector. Uh, very briefly in terms of our first year look, I'm delighted with the progress we have made. Um, uh, uh, probably our number one achievement is to launch our complaints and dispute resolution service, um, which is already fully up and running and, um, probably somewhat sad to say, very busy, uh, in our first year.

Um, and that provides a full ambit of, um, services, um, through, with some very highly skilled trauma informed operators. Um, in April this year, we launched our integrity code, which is sort of the second limb of our, of our being, if you like, which sets minimum standards for the sector. Uh, again, really delighted with the feedback we're receiving around that.

Uh, and we've produced some really outstanding resources for the sector, getting great feedback about how, how helpful they have been. And then very briefly, in terms of challenges, again, um, look, we are very aware of the fiscal constraints, uh, and we believe we can, uh, deliver on our objectives for probably the next two years.

Um, it is fair to say that we do see a period of time if those, if that fiscal envelope doesn't change, where, where our demand will exceed our supply or our ability to meet that demand. But we think we're okay for the next two years. Um, but if there is no change in that envelope, then we will have to adjust our our offerings accordingly.

Um, so I just wanted to be clear about that. The second thing is, um, we really need to now convince our sports, uh, and our recreational sector to adopt our code. It's a voluntary, um, code. We don't impose it on people. We're getting great feedback, but it is quite an exercise for sports to adopt the code.

It takes a lot of work. Um, so that's our big challenge, I suppose, for the next 12 months. Um, and then as we move down our pathway, we really wanna move more and more into our culture change education space, uh, rather than just being the ambulance at the foot of the cliff. Um, so that's the journey we're on. Um, all I wanted to say initially, um, Mr.

Chair, and obviously delighted sake, the questions great. Thank you very much. Thank you for coming today. I suppose the obvious question is, why is the code voluntary? Uh, it's a great question. The, uh, the sense that we had is that if somebody believes, uh, believes in this, these principles, they will adopt it and they'll adopt it with a lot more passion and a lot more vigor than if it's just de pot and imposed centrally.

So we have laws, obviously that provides sort the minimum flaw of rights, if you like, around discrimination, harassment. We wanna take that one level higher, and we thought still believe it's best if somebody comes to this saying, this is right for my sport, rather than I'm being made to do it. Our fear then is that if it would just be a policy stuck in a cupboard, if, if it's, if it's forced on people, if they believe in it.

I mean, I, I think we'd all like to believe that, but having, um, some experience in sport background, you know, it, it might be simpler, people adopt to policies if they have to. And you're talking about culture change sometimes, you know, just so that's just the comment really, I suppose. So what is the uptake? So the uptake at the moment is we're, we're delighted with it.

So bear in mind it only came into giang 3, 3, 4 months ago. So, so far we've had, um, scouts as the largest recreation sector, uh, adopt the code. Um, eSports adopt the code. We have 15, 16 sports on well down the journey. Rebecca May gimme the exact number.

Uh, and excellent feedback now from our major sports. So rugby, football, and cricket have all indicated a de a determination to get there. It does take a lot of, most sports would probably take 12 to 24 months from going. We want to adopt to, we are now ready to adopt because if you, if you can imagine our requirement, because we cover high performance all the way to community, if rugby is going to adopt the code, then every rugby club has to have those policies in place.

But before rugby can say, we are, we are, we are on board, it needs to be really confident it has delivered at all levels. And that's why perhaps you would actually be doing codes a favor by making it mandatory so they don't have to convince all these stakeholders, you know, and because it's like a small parliament.

You've got all your, your, your, your clubs out there and all that, their own their own ideas. And so sometimes if it's just the playing policy, it's come from the top, we've gotta do it. It can actually be a support to codes, especially if it's a code you believe in. Look, I understand that. And I suppose it is now the law that it's voluntary.

So we are required to follow the act as drafted. Um, I mean, if, if we, if I may regret saying this, but when I come back in 12 months time and, and no one's taken it up, it's probably entitled, you're entitled to probably, you know, say, well, it's not working. We we're, we are confident sports are saying we, if you think about it from a sports perspective, the, the, the real question is why wouldn't we?

And it's a really poor look for a sport to say we are not prepared to commit to minimum standards around discrimination, minimum standards around harassment. So we think we'll get Just a thank you, um, then I'll call you once. So did you say the scouts say There was scouts New Zealand? Yes. Yes.

Okay. My suppers to, um, uh, be if, if in the review, and I'm using your words wrongdoings, right? Then that's, you know, that that's telling us something that's over a a a sustained period of time. Things screwed up and nobody took notice.

And then bang, now we're taking notice. And now then, and as you set up the infrastructure for this next shift, um, it seems a little bit light in, in terms of the voluntary, because it's so wrong. It was so wrong. We moved to this point and we were like, we're taking this seriously, and then we back off, we back up and call it a voluntary thing.

How serious is serious then? Because when you use words like a misconduct, uh, harassment, bullying, um, sexual misconduct, when you, you line all those up, we were just, we just passed a bill and, or we were a debating about recently on stalking and harassment that took years for this country to wake up to that.

So when you, those words are showing up in this context. Yeah, I, I don't know whether they're volunt Well, it voluntary doesn't get us quite there. Uh, my, that would be my view and I'm just your, your response to that. Look, as I said, history will, will partly be our judge. Um, one thing I can say is I, I History has already been our judge.

'cause that's how wrongdoings came to be. Yeah, I mean it probably in terms of commission and whether voluntary works, the one thing, uh, just to bear in mind is that we do also have the ability to launch investigations based on the public interest. Mm. So whether or not you adopt the code, uh, there is an overarching right? Um, uh, for Rebecca and her team to receive information which suggests whether or not a sport is a a, a code adopter.

We are going to investigate this sport regardless if it's in the public industry to do so. So we have that backup. We also do have the ability ultimately to work with Sport New Zealand to say, to a sport that chooses not to adopt the code. Ultimately, why are you funding the sport? So, um, we have levers that we can use if we have to, but we'd much rather talk themselves if We can.

I want ask two supplementary, actually just one, one. Can you just remind us how many have voluntarily started? So only two at the moment, but that's which, which one? An now view purely a timing issue. Uh, bearing in mind, we only had the code come in a few months ago. Mm-hmm. So it's scouts and eSports Scouts and eSports. Mm-hmm. And then in terms of the, um, your, your complaints and dispute resolution service that you've now launched, uh, who is, is, what's the process for a sport being part of that?

Can anyone complain from any sport or, or it has to be ones who've signed over the code? Correct. So at the moment, um, we effectively adopted a previous, uh, system that was in place and, and expanded it. So at the moment, every sport has access to that. There may come a time again in the future where we ultimately say, if you want to be able to access our complaints and dispute resolution service and you still refuse to adopt the code, will we make it available to you?

But at the moment, we want participants to be safe. So we want all participants to be able to access our system at the moment. Uh, and after our first year, we were averaging a formal complaint within scope per day, per working day. So, um, there's plenty out there.

Just one. So, um, so bearing in mind that you've only been in operation, well, the code's only been this three or four months. Yes. How, and you've only got, um, scouts and eSports. eSports. How are you knowing that they're there, but how are you gonna evaluate?

How are you, how are you gonna see how well that's working within those codes? Do you want to Yeah, Absolutely. Um, so first of all, I'd say, I think, as Dawn said, irrespective of the code, we have the ability to support any sport and recreation organization through education, uh, receiving complaints.

And, and, and we understand that culture of integrity takes a long time to build. And, and behavioral change does. So to the point about the voluntary, um, that the, the sector, if, if it had been mandatory upfront, everyone probably would've been in breach 'cause they haven't had that time to understand and make those changes. However, uh, and I think you mentioned earlier, Dawn, the legislation just due to be reviewed in 2028, at which point that might be revisited.

So until then, we, uh, progress as, as we are now, uh, with code adopters and, and signatories. And we are able to, I guess if what I think you're asking is assess the value of the code with those organizations. So yeah, through, uh, the interactions we have with them, um, through feedback we get from them through uptake of our, uh, education and our awareness raising, uh, collateral.

Um, and then I think for any agency who receives complaints, if they go up, it's a, it'll sound perverse, but it's a good thing because then, you know, the awareness is higher and, um, people feel safe and they know what to do. So tho those are good indicators for us. So, and, and just on that, so imagining you are sort of, you're holding their hands as they go through Yes, that's right.

Through the adoption of the code, how well are you resourced, assuming we want everyone mm-hmm. To adopt the code, you're gonna hold a lot of hands as we have that culture change. How well resourced are you to do that? How, and, and how are you anticipating that will change?

That's good. Do you mean to take that Too? Yeah, you can start. We have, uh, we difficult. There's no doubt about that. Uh, and we are very conscious that demand will increase as more sports adopt the code and more red sectors of organization adopt the code. We, we can, we feel confident we can make it work with the team we have, uh, and the online resources we are developing for, as I said, the next couple of years, there will come a point where we, if, if, if the growth in complaints we expect to continue continues, there will come a point

where we will need to sit down with the minister and, and say, we, we are approaching a, a, a problem down, but we are okay for the next two years. Supplementary help. Oh, hello Don. Hello. Um, I wanted to know about, uh, fact that you've got a whole lot of volunteers on these organizations who are probably doing your best and have been coming at things from period of angles.

How are you managing that and what's the balance in terms of education versus really serious harm that's being done? Because as we know, investigations can suck up a lot of resource. Yeah, great question. The, the, I think we see ourselves on something of a, of a pathway.

So, um, the first two or three years, it's really crucial. We get our core services right? So that's when I talked about the ambulance, really at the foot of the cliff. So that means we need to be well resourced when complaints come in. We need to make sure victims have the support, have the counseling available, that our investigators are really well trained, et cetera.

So, um, doing our core role, right, is our number one priority. Um, and that includes also, you know, continuing to doing a really strong job in the anti-doping space. Yeah. So core job remains the focus for the first two to three years. We are producing great education resources, but ultimately we will be successful when we get to the point where we go, the core is lockdown, we've got the sector on board, our policies are in place.

Now let's really start focusing more and more on changing hearts and minds, changing attitudes around what is or isn't acceptable in coaching, for example. And we are already doing some of that work, and so is Sport New Zealand. But, um, yeah, we are all excited by the day where we can actually say the core is there.

Um, now let's start moving more into that culture shift. But I, I do think it needs to be a progression. If we start at that end, we'll let people down who need our support. Now, New question, Warren. Thank you. Um, can I turn to, um, complaints, resolution and investigations part? Um, so you said you've been unfortunately busy, but is that, does that include maybe historic issues?

And this is maybe a wash up of a lot of that, and do you expect that to taper off into the future? Or is it that, sorry, I'll, I'll spit them all out at once, but is that, um, so is this because people are becoming more aware of integrity and are coming forward, and then how do you, how do you include in any data, um, issues that might happen outside of your organization?

So I'm thinking, you know, particularly about the fraud and breach of trust, which may go straight to the police and you may not even be aware of that. You capture any of that. Yeah, yeah. Okay. I'll start. I think it, you're talking about historic matters Absolutely. That form some of the complaints we get and that's appropriate.

Um, and often and more often than not, actually, they're linked to current behavior as well. So that, that, that can be a, a continuum of, of a complaint. And in many of our matters, we get more than one complainant, um, which won't surprise people. Uh, but that, that captures anything up to this point. Uh, for code signatories, only matter only breaches of the code can be dealt with once someone has signed the code.

So we can't go back in time. Oh, okay. But, um, but we, we can still do, uh, as, as Don said, we've still got the power to investigate now. And we are, we've, we've got a number of investigations, a number of complaints, and we are certainly, um, holding the hands of the sector as we go. For sure. To, to clarify Peter, too, we, we, so we we're receiving on, on, on average one complaint day in scope.

Um, the ones that out are out of scope, for example, may be an employment dispute where the law says that must be dealt with by the employment court, um, employment relations authority. So there are not everything that's happening in the world of integrity and sport and recreation comes to us because it might be employment related, might, as you rightly say, it might be police related.

It might be SFO related. Um, so our ambit is sort of sits in a fairly clear box. Okay. I'm a bit confused now. Um, so the, these complaints received already are just from scouts? No, no, no, No. Sorry. No. They, they cover 70 different organizations.

Our complaints so far, we've had from 70 different organizations 7 0, 7 0, 7 0. Yep. Yep. Um, so when we talk about scouts and eSports, they're the only ones at the moment who can make complaints about breaches of the code. 'cause they're the only ones who've signed the code right at the moment. So, so if you, the, the ones we've had to date, because up until June 30, we didn't have anyone signing the code.

So the ones that this, this committee's, um, here to discuss, they're all, um, non code complaints Okay. That, that we've been dealing with. And, and just to, to pick up your last question as well, we have, uh, good relationships and in some cases, um, memorandum understanding with agencies such as Sport New Zealand police in development with, uh, serious port office, TAB, and others, so that we can share that information.

Oh, great. And, and I think it's fair to say that if, if it does tend to touch us, we hear about it. So, but that's encouraging. Very Good. Thank you. Supplemental on that though, those 70 Yes. Um, Sectors that you've had complaints from, is, is there a commonality sort of groupings in terms of the complaints that are coming through that you've noticing?

Yeah, I, I, I've got the statistics here. They, um, uh, perhaps sadly, but, but perhaps not unexpectedly, the, um, majority of complainants are female and, and relate to, um, allegations against females, against women. Um, interestingly, uh, the majority of our complaints, approximately 65% are out of community community sport.

35% are more on that high performance, uh, area. And far and away the, the largest, uh, area of complaints are in that participant protection. So bullying, um, abuse, sexual misconduct, far and away probably taking up, um, just over half of all complaints come in with that type of theme, sadly.

Right. And how, how many complaints in total, How many, Sorry, complaints in total? Complaints in total? Uh, 283 in, uh, last year. Okay. What's, what, Sorry. Oh, I actually gonna ask just off there. Yeah. Like, so what, what does the participation gimme an example Of participation Yeah.

Of that, of that particular 280, you know. Um, So any, an example may be at that the community level, a uh, a 13-year-old swimmer who feels very uncomfortable about how their coach is talking to her. Uh, and, um, either raises it with a friend or raises it with a parent who rings our hotline and says, what do I do?

Um, that would be a, a standard type of complaint. What, do you have any ethnicity stats and what, um, reflections do you make on those In terms of the complaints? Uh, we collect them. I don't have them here. We can get back to you on those. Are There any themes that strike you that you know, that have struck you that you can share now?

Um, what I can say is through the establishment phase, we did a lot of consultation, uh, in the community, um, through, uh, with Maori and Pacifica. And, uh, racism was particularly prevalent. Um, and that, that has, uh, informed a lot of the services we provide.

We have, um, culturally appropriate services for that reason. And, and, and that we have an example at the moment where we're working with, um, a Tik based organization to support that, but it's not only going to be limited to al Maori, uh, we have people we can work with around all ethnicities as well.

So, um, very determined and it says in our legislation, we need to be able to respond to, and we do it anyway 'cause it's the right thing, uh, um, the rights and obligations in, in terms of Maori. So something we, we have woven into our, our services. Cool. I would be interested in that, those details. Yes. And just up on, on that stuff.

Um, so when you, when you deal with a complaint and you reach resolution, how do you know that it's been successful? Like, you know, what sort of ongoing monitoring or feedback do you get? I mean, I, I think the, the answer to that is that if there is resolution, unless there is as part of that and agreed to check in, we will step away.

And, and that's simply a resource related issue. So, uh, if, if in the example we talked about, if there's some education given to the coach and, um, parents who are made clear that if, you know, there's a continuation or the, or the, or the youth is made clear, there's a continuation, raise it.

I don't believe we would've the resources to keep going back. Uh, is everything fine? Is everything fine? Is everything fine? We just don't, I mean, we are a very small organization Yeah. Dealing with a massive sector. Yeah. Um, but we would make sure there is that continual ability to come back to us. Yeah. But you would also, I imagine, be monitoring if there was a pattern of repeat offending.

Oh, absolutely. Yeah. So you would be keeping an eye on that. Yeah. And that also we have that ability too for our own, um, as I said, public interest investigation. So if we started to see that we would absolutely have the ability to go back to that sport and say we are concerned, um, and, uh, into our own process. Good. A supplement on that, I think you've largely answered it, but just to double check.

So that in terms of the complaints and dispute to this that you have, is that, is that why in your, uh, performance reports for your report, the response times to the complaint as opposed to resolution of the complaint? Yeah, So that's what we need to get better at developing this year. Uh, I mean, in our first year it was just get it established, um, and we totally accept that we now need to start, you know, we're established or underway.

How are we monitoring our response times? Are we, are we up to speed? Are we improving? Um, so that's very much a key part of our work this year. Okay. Thanks Helen. Hi. We have been, we've just been doing stalking legislation. Mm-hmm. And obviously there's a, um, an awareness, um, that a lot of these, um, complaints people are afraid of continuing through it.

Is this a, a process where there's any room for not complaints where you can see a pattern? Or does everybody get natural justice and therefore people making a complaint are going to be, uh, fearful perhaps of making that complaint? I'm not saying you shouldn't do that, but what's the, what's the ground rules in terms of seeing those patterns of behavior, um, given that you might get a young swimmer who doesn't necessarily want to say that the coach is doing this or they're in competitive situation?

Yeah, again, a really good question. The, i I think it's probably a little early to say. Yeah, I, I think we are flexible enough to both look after the participant and deal with the issue, but also have sufficient flexibility in our legislation to actually go to the sport and say, we have a much bigger, we are seeing a much broader issue here.

Right. And indeed, we've already done that at a, at a high level with one sport, um, you know, had discussions with the board about trends that we aren't overly delighted about. Um, so, uh, I think we have the flexibility to do that. And you might have more flexibility than many other agencies.

A hundred percent because we are not dealing at, generally not dealing with crime. We're dealing at that level below. Yeah. So I think that's exactly right. Okay. Thanks. I just add, oh, sorry. Just our legislation also includes, um, pause around if you discrimination. So if someone is being discriminated against for making a complaint, uh, and they think it's affecting their selection or the way they're treated, uh, similar to the protected disclosures that we have, similar kind of wording in our legislation as an extra protection

and also with the code, one of the minimum standards is about reporting matters of serious concern to us, even if the sport are dealing with it themselves. So we do get that overall sort of helicopter view, if you like, of, of what's coming in. We do, Yeah. Supplementary more. Yeah. I'm just, um, wondering About the sensitivity of some of the information that you are receiving and, and the need to protect that.

But I also wonder is there, um, potential in the, say if someone was thinking about employing a new coach or whatever, can they come to you and say, are there any red flags? Are you able to alert them to anything that you may have dealt with? Um, the appropriate, sorry, the appropriate, uh, I guess steps, and this won't be surprising to many of you, is, is around obviously police betting your normal employment checks and references and that sort of thing, but you pointed out that we, we can't, uh, give a, a green tick for prospective coach or, or employee.

However, I think over time, once we've built up some of that intelligence and insights, um, we can probably take more proactive steps around making sure things are in place in, in, in the sector so that, uh, sports are much better at, um, at protecting their participants. We did look at, um, when we were setting up the, the commission whether we could effectively have some sort of central agency, if you like, for protection of participants, but there were so many privacy law, uh, other implications of that.

Um, one thing we are really pleased about though is that our, if you adopt the code, uh, you adopt our child safeguarding, uh, policies which require, for example, vetting. So, uh, if you're going to actually say, um, yes, um, squash New Zealand as part of our, uh, code, then every squash coach will need to be vetted before they take on that role. Yeah. But, But police vetting is quite different to the complaints that you are receiving. Yeah, yeah.

It's not the panacea. I totally accept that. No, It's, it's a balance Motivat. Mm. Sorry. In, in the same vein, um, Don, which is you just said, um, we're not dealing with crime and, but one might be how do you, how do you assess, what's your threshold of, you know, when would be the point at which you, you might say to who somebody that's in this, um, complaints proceedings, um, it might be time to go there. Do you Hand that one to the former police officer?

Yes. Great. Straight. No, uh, really good and important question. As Don said, we, we deal in the sub criminal, but we've got some really experienced, uh, some police and certainly some legal expertise who can very quickly recognize when, when it's something hits a criminal threshold and some really good relationships, uh, and police in particular, DIA, uh, there, there are some others and, and who we can talk to directly to decide what to do with it.

And we've, I think if I had in front of me, I referred about 40 cases, not, not just to police mm-hmm. But to other agencies. There are, they're listed in our legislation. You question Scott, Uh, on, on doping Christian. 'cause I see you've picked up the anti-doping responsibilities. Yes. Mm-hmm. Um, how are you, how are you managing that?

What's the, what's the ability to, uh, keep on doing that, which is, you know, as we know, it's, it's a big issue. Mm. And a lot to manage. Yeah. I, I, I've been really delighted if it's, uh, actually very proud of the fact that we've brought, um, almost two entities together.

Um, one, one burgeoning in terms of integrity, integrity unit and, and, and, and our drug free sport New Zealand brought them together and I think very quickly created a really cohesive team. Um, and, and I applaud my CEO for the work she's done in that regard. The, I I've not received any suggestion from anyone in our sporting community that that's led to a lack of focus on anti-doping.

We're still doing must do the statutory number of tests that were required. We're still doing the education that's required. We are looking to refine that education so that we get the chance to educate an integrity and an, an anti-doping at the same time, or often in the same opportunity, or, or, or separate. We're just working through how we best do that. But, um, we're still hugely committed to, to, um, catching as many, well, our focus isn't on catching the cheeks.

Our focus is on educating people not to dope, but obviously we need to catch dopes as well. Uh, and, um, yeah, I'm really confident we haven't stepped away from that at all. Mm-hmm. So I was really, really interested in anti-doping around the costs, because the commission obviously do your testing and you're a user pay.

So I'd be interested on what those tests actually do cost the commission or as a body user pace. But my question, what I then wanted to go to is, we've got a very high profile boxer mm-hmm. Who at the moment is under a cloud and nobody knows the result of that.

But when we do, you step into a situation like that because integrity of boxing becomes, uh, people are looking at that, what, what's happening there? Can you step into that situation, even though that's happening somewhere offshore? The answer to the second question is simply no.

Um, that professional boxing does its own, uh, testing under its own system. Mm-hmm. Um, the box in New Zealand have signed our sports anti-doping rules, but that's more at the amateur community level. If you're a professional boxer, you are in the world of professional box. And we have no influence over that, unfortunately.

Uh, in terms of user page, yeah. Do you wanna explain? Sure. Um, I can, um, so we've got about 30 organizations who, uh, get user pay services from us. And they are, um, sports like, um, New Zealand, rugby's, one of them, um, and other international bodies and other anti-doping organizations around the world.

But that's run outta cost neutral, um, on a cost neutral basis. And, and I think the total cost might even be in the annual report, but I won't take it out yet. We will definitely come back to you on that. But they, they are costs that, um, we, we've sort of built a model whereby what we charge reflects not only the, the cost of the test, but some of the human resource involved in collecting it.

'cause we use our, our sample collection personnel and we have a, we can only use, um, for example, a lab in Sydney. Uh, 'cause that's a wider accredited lab. There isn't one in New Zealand. So we're now send our samples to Sydney, uh, most of the time in some cases Europe Ary. Um, how many of the six violations involved intentional vaping?
